coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-confined CoP-CoO nanoheterostructure with strong interfacial synergy triggered the robust hydrogen evolution from ammonia borane
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by ELSEVIER B.V. and Science Press. All rights reserved.

coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Han
Cheng, Yaojia
Wang, Boyang
Wang, Yao
Wu, Min
Li, Weidong
Liu, Baozhong
Lu, Siyu
coredata.dc:description: Ammonia borane (NH3BH3, AB) is promising for chemical hydrogen storage; however, current systems for rapid hydrogen production are limited by the expensive noble metal catalysts required for AB hydrolysis. Here we report the design and synthesis of a highly efficient and robust non-noble-metal catalyst for the hydrolysis of AB at 298 K (TOF = 89.56molH2 min−1 molCo −1). Experiments and density functional theory calculations were performed to explore the catalyst’s hybrid nanoparticle heterostructure and its catalytic mechanism. The catalyst comprised nitrogen-doped carbon dots confining CoO and CoP, and exhibited strong interface-induced synergistic catalysis for AB hydrolysis that effectively decreased the energy barriers for the dissociation of both AB and water molecules. The co-doping of N and P introduced numerous defects, and further regulated the reactivity of the carbon layers. The heterogeneous interface design technique presented here provides a new strategy for developing efficient and inexpensive non-noble-metal catalysts that may be applicable in other fields related to energy catalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nano-heterostructure
Interface engineering
Ammonia borane
Hydrolysis mechanism
Hydrogen

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Hydrothermal formation of NCDs, and their characterization: (b) TEM image (inset: HRTEM image), (c) size distribution, (d) XRD pattern, (e) FTIR spectrum, (f) excitation-emission PL spectrum, (g) UV–vis, PL emission, and PL excitation spectra (inset: optical photograph in aqueous solution under 365 nm light).

(a) XRD patterns of Co/NCDs, Co–Co3O4/NCDs, and CoP–CoO/NCDs, (b) SEM image of CoP–CoO/NCDs. Further characterisation of CoP–CoO/NCDs: (c) TEM image (inset: size distribution), (d, e) high-magnification TEM images, (f) HRTEM image, (g) element mapping images.
(a) Raman spectra and (b) F–IR spectra of Co/NCDs, Co–Co3O4/NCDs, and CoP–CoO/NCDs. (c) XPS survey spectra and (d) Co 2p, (e) P 2p, and (f) N 1s high-resolution spectra of CoP–CoO/NCDs.

(a) Hydrogen evolution from AB hydrolysis catalysed by Co/NCDs, Co–Co3O4/NCDs, and CoP–CoO/NCDs at 298 K, and (b) corresponding hydrogen evolution rates. (c) Hydrogen evolution from AB hydrolysis catalysed by CoP–CoO/NCDs at different temperatures and the corresponding Arrhenius plot. (d) Stability test of AB hydrolysis catalysed by CoP–CoO/NCDs at 298 K. (e) Hydrogen evolution from AB hydrolysis catalysed by CoP–CoO/NCDs-y (y = i, ii, iii, iv, v) and CoP–CoO/NCDs, and (f) their XRD patterns.

(a) Hydrogen evolution from AB hydrolysis catalysed by CoP–CoO/NCDs, CoP–CoO/CDs, and CoP x /NCDs. (b) XRD patterns of CoP–CoO/CDs and CoP x /NCDs. (c) N and P co-doping and interface effect for improving AB hydrolysis. (d) Energy profiles of AB and H2O molecules dissociated on Co(111), CoO(200), and CoO/CoP surfaces. The three configurations along the reaction path for each surface are reactants, transition states (TS), and products. The Co, P, B, N, and H atoms are represented by blue, purple, green, grey, and white circles. To distinguish O in CoO from that in H2O, the former is represented by the light blue circle and the latter is presented by the red circle. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-confined CoP-CoO nanoheterostructure with strong interfacial synergy triggered the robust hydrogen evolution from ammonia borane

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310014, Zhejiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310014, Zhejiang, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A unique CoP–CoO nanoheterostructure which achieves simultaneous decomposition of H2O and NH3BH3 molecules is successfully constructed through interfacial engineering and exhibits excellent catalytic activity for the hydrolysis of NH3BH3.

Ammonia borane (NH3BH3, AB) is promising for chemical hydrogen storage; however, current systems for rapid hydrogen production are limited by the expensive noble metal catalysts required for AB hydrolysis. Here we report the design and synthesis of a highly efficient and robust non-noble-metal catalyst for the hydrolysis of AB at 298 K (TOF = 89.56molH2 min−1 molCo −1). Experiments and density functional theory calculations were performed to explore the catalyst’s hybrid nanoparticle heterostructure and its catalytic mechanism. The catalyst comprised nitrogen-doped carbon dots confining CoO and CoP, and exhibited strong interface-induced synergistic catalysis for AB hydrolysis that effectively decreased the energy barriers for the dissociation of both AB and water molecules. The co-doping of N and P introduced numerous defects, and further regulated the reactivity of the carbon layers. The heterogeneous interface design technique presented here provides a new strategy for developing efficient and inexpensive non-noble-metal catalysts that may be applicable in other fields related to energy catalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Constructing and precisely engineering catalytic interfaces can efficiently boost their intrinsic activity. In comparison to monophase structures, heterostructures tend to have stronger interfacial effects, such as modified electronic structure, electronic transfer capability, and electron density of activity sites, which make them more reactive [1–4]. Interface engineering also allows the catalyst structure to be designed specifically for a particular catalytic mechanism, which can improve its performance by reducing barriers to the reaction.

Recently, hydrogen evolution from chemical storage materials has gained wide interest [5,6]. A promising example is the hydrolysis of ammonia borane (NH3BH3, AB) owing to its mild conditions and high hydrogen release [7,8]. However, the current best catalysts contain Pt or Ru [9–11], the high cost and limited reserves of which greatly inhibit large-scale use. Therefore, the development of highly active and stable non-noble-metal catalysts, including oxides [12,13], sulfides [14], carbides [15] and phosphides [16,17], is crucial to AB becoming a widely used storage material. Transition-metal phosphides can efficiently catalyse hydrogen evolution, owing to their similarity to hydrogenase [18,19], but are limited by low reactivity and poor cyclic stability. To maximize their efficiency, their structure and composition need to be optimized to increase the amount of exposed active sites. Structural design must also increase their durability. Therefore, surface and interface engineering can help construct efficient heterogeneous catalysts, where strong interface synergy can enhance the metal phosphide’s activity [20,21].

Given the critical role of the interface structure, precisely designing heterostructures specifically for AB’s hydrolysis mechanism should lead to efficient catalysts. The rapid hydrolysis of AB on a suitable metal catalyst progresses first with the adsorption of water and AB molecules (i.e., reactants) at catalytic active sites. Their subsequent dissociation forms various reaction intermediates (e.g., BH3OH*, BH2(OH)2*, and BH(OH)3*), which go on to generate and release H2 (see Table S1 in the Electronic Support Information (ESI)) [12,16,18,22]. The main rate-determining step is the dissociation of water and AB molecules, and reducing its energy barrier is crucial for improving hydrogen evolution efficiency [22,23]. Many excellent water dissociation promoters, such as metal oxides, sulfides, and hydroxides, have previously been used to cleave HO–H bonds and generate H*. Metal phosphides have also been demonstrated to effectively adsorb AB molecules and activate B–N bonds to further reduce the reaction energy barrier [17,18]. Inspired by the above, we posit that the construction of novel, nano-scale heterostructures of metal oxide and metal phosphide with strong interface synergy would simultaneously promote the activation of AB molecules and the decomposition of water molecules, thereby greatly promoting the hydrolysis of AB.

This is the first report of a novel CoO–CoP nano-heterostructure synthesized through interfacial engineering (Fig. 1 , see Experimental Section). To further enhance the structural stability of the designed catalyst, carbon dots (CDs) with large specific surface area, good electrical conductivity, and excellent hydrophilicity were used as carrier materials, owing to their strong space-confining effect with metal nanoparticles (NPs) [24–27]. Nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) confined hybrid CoP–CoO NPs in a structure (labelled CoP–CoO/NCDs) that showed excellent catalytic activity (TOF = 89.56molH2 min−1 molCo −1) at 298 K, better than almost all reported non-noble-metal catalysts and even some noble-metal catalysts. Such excellent performance reveals the potential superiority of the CoO–CoP nano-heterostructure. The novel confining effect of the NCDs gave the CoP–CoO/NCDs good stability. Experiments and density functional theory (DFT) calculations further verified that the high activity was because of the novel interface-induced synergistic catalysis mechanism, which simultaneously facilitated decomposition of both water and AB molecules.

Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, AR; Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd.), citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, GR; Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., China), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, AR; Tianjin HengXing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China),sodiumhypophosphite (NaH2PO2, 99%; Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd.), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR; Sino Pharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China), ammonia borane (AB, 97%; Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China) and deionized (DI) water are purchased from commercial suppliers. All of the chemicals are used as received without any further purification.

Synthesis of CDs: A one-step hydrothermal method prepared the NCDs. Briefly, 4 mmol C6H8O7·H2O and 1.6 mmol C2H8N2 were added to 30 mL deionized water. After blending, the solution was transferred to a 60 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, filtering through a 0.22 μm membrane removed impurities from the product, which appeared as a reddish-brown liquid. Freeze-drying produced NCDs powder, which was stored at 4 °C until redispersed in deionized water for further characterization. Non-nitrogen-doped CDs were also similarly synthesized without using C2H8N2.

Synthesis of CoP–CoO/NCDs: First, Co/NCDs NPs were synthesized. A typical procedure involved dissolving 296 mg Co(NO3)2·6H2O in the obtained NCDs solution. After ultrasonication for 30 min, the resulting homogeneous solution was transferred to a 60 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, excess liquid was removed by rotary evaporation, and the product was freeze-dried. The resulting dark brown powder was annealed in argon at 600 °C for 4 h at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. Samples of Co–Co3O4/NCDs and CoP–CoO/NCDs were then obtained via interface engineering, which involved injecting oxygen and phosphorus to construct different metal nano-heterostructures. Briefly, the Co/NCDs powder was exposed to relatively hot air (200 °C), which ensured efficient oxygen injection. After 22 h, Co–Co3O4/NCDs with a Co–Co3O4 heterostructure were obtained. This was further converted into a novel CoP–CoO structure by further phosphorus injection. Briefly, two quartz boats containing sodium hypophosphate and Co–Co3O4/NCDs were placed upstream and downstream of a tubular furnace, respectively. Injection was performed at 300 °C for 2 h, using argon as a carrier gas, with heating of 2 °C min−1. Finally, CoP–CoO/NCDs was obtained. The novel CoP–CoO heterostructure was modulated by adjusting the ratio of Co–Co3O4/NCDs to sodium hypophosphate: mass ratios of 1:1, 1:3, 1:5, 1:6, 1:7, and 1:9 gave products labelled CoP–CoO/NCDs-i, CoP–CoO/NCDs-ii, CoP–CoO/NCDs-iii, CoP–CoO/NCDs, CoP–CoO/NCDs-iv, and CoP–CoO/NCDs-v, respectively.
Synthesis of CoP–CoO/CDs: Similar to the method for that for CoP–CoO/NCDs, except with NCDs replaced by non-nitrogen-doped CDs, producing CoP–CoO/CDs.
CoP x /NCDs: CoP x /NCDs was also synthesized using a similar method to that for CoP–CoO/NCDs, except much more sodium hypophosphate was added than that used for Co–Co3O4/NCDs.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies were performed on a Rigaku Dmax-3C X-ray diffraction (Cu Kα radiation, λ = 0.15418 nm, 40 kV, 40 mA). The 2θ angles were scanned from 5° to 90° at 5° min−1. The acquired diffraction data were processed with Jade software. The morphology of materials was characterized through emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS MERLIN Compact). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping were observed on an FEI Talos, F200S instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a PHI quantera SXM spectrometer. Raman spectra were obtained using a LabRAM HR Evolution (HORIBA). Infrared spectroscopy was performed on an IR Spectrometer NEXUS 470. Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES) was carried out in Agilent 5110. UV–vis was acquired on a TU-1810PC. PL emission and PL excitation spectra were acquired on an RF-6000 (Shimadzu, Japan).

The ab-initio calculations were performed using the projector augmented wave (PAW) formalism of density functional theory (DFT) as implemented in the Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP). The generalized gradient approximation formulated by Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerhof (PBE) for exchange–correlation function was employed. An energy cutoff of 400 eV for the plane-wave expansion of the wavefunctions was used for all the calculation. For geometry optimization, the atomic coordinates were relaxed until the Hellmann-Feynman forces were less than 0.01 eV/Å in order to investigate the mechanism for the very different HER efficiencies. Three structural models (Co(111), CoO(200) and CoO/CoP) were employed to calculate the energy profiles along the reaction of both NH3BH3 and H2O.

A one-step hydrothermal method produced NCDs from citric acid and ethylenediamine (Fig. 2 a). The reaction’s main processes were dehydration polymerization between the reactants and subsequent carbonization [28]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the NCDs (Fig. 2b, c) show them uniformly distributed, with an average size of 2.08 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in the inset of Fig. 2(b) indicates the presence of well-resolved lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.207 nm, corresponding to the (100) planar spacing of graphene carbon [29]. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern of the NCDs (Fig. 2d) shows a broad peak centred at 22.4°, which corresponds to highly crystalline carbon [30]. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum (Fig. 2e) shows stretching vibrations of COH (3385 cm−1), CH (2918 cm−1), CO/CN (1652 cm−1), and CC (1545 cm−1) functional groups, indicating the formation of polyaromatic structures and the presence of oxygen/nitrogen-containing groups. The surface chemical states of the NCDs were also studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Fig. S1), which identified three typical peaks of C 1s (285 eV), O 1s (400 eV), and N 1s (531 eV). The surfaces of the NCDs were modified by abundant hydrophilic functional groups, which facilitated strong complexation between them and the metal cations. The UV–vis spectra of the NCDs (Fig. 2f) show absorption bands at 242 and 335 nm, which were attributable to the π → π* transition of CC and the n → π* transition of CO and CN, respectively [31]. The NCDs showed optimal blue luminescence centred at 430 nm with significant excitation-dependent photoluminescence due to their surface state affecting the band gap (Fig. 2f, g) [32].

Given the good hydrophilicity, large specific surface area, and excellent electron transport ability of NCDs, they were selected as an ideal carrier material to synthesize the unique two-phase interfacial structure (Fig. 1, see Experimental Section). Cobalt NPs confined by NCDs (Co/NCDs) were synthesized successfully by simply calcining the precursor under an argon flow. The Co–Co3O4 nano-heterostructure was then constructed via local phase transition from metal to metal oxide (Co–Co3O4/NCDs) during oxygen injection. Finally, CoP–CoO/NCDs with an interfacial CoO and CoP structure were successfully obtained by further phosphorus injection; Fig. 3 (a) shows their XRD patterns. The cubic cobalt phase (JCPDS Card No. 15-0806) is clearly detected in both Co/NCD and Co–Co3O4/NCDs, while cubic cobaltosic oxide (JCPDS Card No. 42-1467) is only be identified in the latter. Meanwhile, some peaks assigned to cubic cobaltous oxide (JCPDS Card No. 48-1719) and orthorhombic phosphating cobalt (JCPDS Card No. 29-0497) can be observed in CoP–CoO/NCDs, indicating the successful construction of a CoO–CoP nano-heterostructure. Strafing electron microscopy (Fig. 3b, S2) showed CoP–CoO/NCDs as comprising many NPs uniformly distributed on the surface of the matrix. Further TEM imaging (Fig. 3c) indicates these NPs have a wide size distribution from 40 and 80 nm, with an average value of 58.82 nm. The high-magnification TEM image (Fig. 3d) of CoP–CoO/NCDs displays NPs confined tightly in the matrix; further examination revealed the NPs to be tightly covered by external carbon layers (green arrows in Fig. 3e). The HRTEM image (Fig. 3f) of CoP–CoO/NCDs shows clear lattice fringes of CoO (2 0 0) and CoP (2 1 1), confirming a polycrystalline structure composed of CoO and CoP (the crystal interface is highlighted by a yellow dotted line). Moreover, outer lattice fringes were also observed and attributed to graphene (0 0 2). Element mapping images (Fig. 3g) of CoP–CoO/NCDs show uniformly distributed elemental C, O, N, P, and Co, which indicates successful introduction of elemental N, O, and P into the carbon layers. Meanwhile, the elemental Co and P within inner NPs appear surrounded by elemental N and C, validating that CoP–CoO hybrid NPs were covered by external graphene layers. This novel configuration arose owing mainly to the strong coordination interaction between NCDs and metal cations, which further induced the formation of the unique core–shell structure through the space-confining effect of the surface NCDs during calcination [27].

Raman spectra of the Co/NCDs, Co–Co3O4/NCDs, and CoP–CoO/NCDs are presented in Fig. 4 (a). All samples showed typical D bands (at 1336 cm−1) and G bands (at 1587 cm−1) of carbon atoms. The intensity ratios of the bands (I D/I G) were 0.97, 1.01, and 1.04, respectively, for each sample. The increasing ratio indicates increasingly disordered carbon components in these samples as oxygen and nitrogen were injected. The highest I D/I G ratio of CoP–CoO/NCDs may have been due to the presence of comparatively more exposed edges from the local oxidation of the carbon layers and more structure defects derived from the doping of N, O, and P atoms. Some bands (at 469, 512, and 673 cm−1) attributed to Co3O4 were detected in the Raman spectra of Co–Co3O4/NCDs, indicating a local phase transition from Co to Co3O4 during oxygen injection, consistent with the above XRD results [33]. The disappearance of these peaks in the Raman spectra of CoP–CoO/NCDs suggests that some phosphorus had been successfully introduced into the metallic hybrid NPs through partial replacement of oxygen, which further led to the formation of the CoO–CoP nano-heterostructure. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 4b) of Co/NCDs, Co–Co3O4/NCDs, and CoP–CoO/NCDs show the retention of abundant functional groups on their surfaces, which greatly increased their dispersibility in water.

The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 4c) of CoP–CoO/NCDs further confirm the presence of C, N, O, Co, and P surface species. The corresponding high-resolution Co 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 4d) show peaks at 781.9 and 778.9 eV (Co 2p 3/2), which can be assigned to Co2+ in CoO and Co–P in CoP, respectively [34]. The peaks at 786.5 and 803.2 eV are shake-up satellite peaks. Moreover, the binding energies of the P 2p peaks (Fig. 4e) located at 129.7, 130.7, and 134.9 eV correspond to P 2p 3/2, P 2p 1/2 in CoP, and surface oxidized P species, respectively [35]. These results further indicate the successful construction of a CoO–CoP nano-heterostructure. The N 1s spectra (Fig. 4f) of CoP–CoO/NCDs show two main N types: graphitic (400.4 eV, 2.01%) and pyrrolic (399.3 eV, 0.34%). The introduction of N is established as an effective way to regulate the electronic structure of carbon–metal composites to improve their catalytic activity [24,36]. The C 1s and O 1s spectra (Fig. S3) of CoP–CoO/NCDs also indicate the existence of various surface functional groups, consistent with the results above. A peak of Co–O from CoO was also observed at 530.4 eV.

The catalytic activities of Co/NCDs, Co–Co3O4/NCDs, and CoP–CoO/NCDs were evaluated for the hydrolysis of alkaline AB (See ESI for the detailed testing process). The results in Fig. 5 (a and b) show, as expected, that CoP–CoO/NCDs required the shortest time (about 1.5 min) to complete hydrogen evolution at 298 K; Co/NCDs required the longest time. Fig. 5(b) shows their hydrogen generation specific rate (r B): CoP–CoO/NCDs exhibited the highest value, r B = 6117mLH2 min−1 gCat. −1. The TOF of CoP–CoO/NCDs was further calculated as 89.56molH2 min−1 molCo −1 based on the amount of cobalt in the catalyst (Table S2). This is superior to almost all reported non-noble-metal catalysts and even some noble metal catalysts (Table S3). Compared with Co/NCDs, the metal nano-heterostructure within Co–Co3O4/NCDs and CoP–CoO/NCDs improved their catalytic activities [12]. The outstanding performance of CoP–CoO/NCDs indicates that the novel two-phase heterostructure of CoP and CoO is particularly advantageous for AB hydrolysis, which could occur at different temperatures (Fig. 5c, Fig. S4). Increasing the reaction temperature clearly promoted hydrolysis by speeding up the transport of ions or molecules. In particular, the hydrolysis of AB catalysed by CoP–CoO/NCDs completed rapidly with an ultrahigh TOF of 265.6molH2 min−1 molCo −1 at 318 K. An Arrhenius plot made using the different temperature data gave an apparent activation energy (Ea) of 41 kJ mol−1 (See ESI), which is comparable to or lower than those of many previous noble-metal and non-noble-metal NP systems (Table S3). The stability of AB hydrolysis catalysed by CoP–CoO/NCDs was further investigated at 298 K (Fig. 5d). The NPs still maintained high catalytic activity after five cycles. TEM images (Fig. S5) of the used NPs showed that their structure remained intact, with no significant agglomeration. The slight attenuation of activity might have been due to the accumulation of metaborate on the catalyst surface, which created amorphous surface phases [12,33]. The CoP–CoO/NCDs-y (y = i − v) series with similar two-phase interfacial structures obtained by adjusting the conditions for phosphorus injection (Fig. 5e, f; see Experimental Section) all showed excellent catalytic activities (completion of AB hydrolysis within 4 min), which further confirms the potentially superiority of the CoP–CoO nano-heterostructure for AB hydrolysis. The optimization of the activity of these NPs also implies that interface engineering can effectively improve the intrinsic activity of heterogeneous catalysts.

The origins of the high catalytic activity were investigated to explore the relationship between activity and composition or structure. First, a non-nitrogen-doped sample with the two-phase interfacial structure of CoP and CoO (CoP–CoO/CDs NPs) was synthesized using non-nitrogen doped CDs as the carrier material (Fig. 6 b, see Experimental Section). Fig. 6(a) shows that the activity for AB hydrolysis was decreased somewhat, compared with that of CoP–CoO/NCDs, thus demonstrating that the introduction of nitrogen enhanced the activity of CoP–CoO/NCDs for AB hydrolysis. Nitrogen, as a heterogeneous atom into a carbon matrix, generally causes defects, which will disorganize the original charge distribution on the surface of the carbon layers [37–39]. As two opposite non-metallic dopants for graphene, nitrogen, and phosphorus used together strongly affect the valence orbital energy level of the adjacent carbon atoms, and so work together to activate those adjacent carbon atoms and improve the activity of the whole carbon material (Fig. 6c) [40,41]. Phosphorus injection into a carbon framework has been reported to aid the adsorption of AB molecules and promote their hydrolysis [20]. The role of the novel CoP–CoO nano-heterostructure in AB hydrolysis was also investigated. The NCDs-confined CoP x NPs (CoP x /NCDs) were synthesized by injecting excess phosphorus (Fig. 6b, see Experimental Section), ensuring that the CoO phase was completely replaced by CoP x phases. This eliminated the CoP–CoO nano-heterostructure. The resultant great reduction in catalytic activity (Fig. 6a) suggests that a strong interfacial synergy in the CoP–CoO nano-heterostructure is key to the enhancement of AB hydrolysis (Fig. 6c).

DFT calculations clarified the mechanism of synergistic catalysis of the interfacestructure of CoP and CoO. (see Experimental Section for computational details). As a reference, a structural model of cobalt metal (Co(111)) was first employed to calculate the energy profiles of the reactions of AB and H2O. Fig. 6(d) shows very weak adsorption by both molecules on the Co(111) surface: that of H2O is only −0.15 eV, whereas the positive value for AB indicates an unfavourable reaction. This is consistent with pure Co (Co/NCDs, see Fig. 5a) showing the least hydrogen evolution efficiency among the tested samples. Structural models of CoO/CoP and CoO(200) were also employed to study the promotion effect of the CoP–CoO nano-heterostructure. Fig. 6(d) shows that the energy barrier for the dissociation of H2O from H2O* to HO* on the CoO(200) surface (1.34 eV) is smaller than that on the CoO/CoP (2.09 eV) or Co(111) (2.45 eV) surface, indicating that, during the hydrolysis of AB, CoO promoted water dissociation; therefore, its removal from CoP–CoO/NCDs will inevitably lead to the poor catalytic activity of CoP x /NCDs. Furthermore, the energy barrier for the dissociation of AB from NH3BH3* to NH3BH2* on the CoO/CoP surface (2.48 eV) is smaller than that on the CoO(200) surface (2.81 eV), indicating that the introduction of CoP and the formation of CoO/CoP interfaces effectively reduced the barrier of AB dissociation, and accelerated the generation of hydrogen. Overall, the DFT calculations reveal an interface-induced synergistic catalysis mechanism that achieves simultaneous decomposition of water and AB molecules. This is consistent with the high catalytic activity of CoP–CoO/NCDs.

In conclusion, we successfully designed and constructed a CoP–CoO nano-heterostructure (CoP–CoO/NCDs) specific for the hydrolysis mechanism of AB via interface engineering. The resulting CoP–CoO/NCDs showed strong catalytic activity (TOF = 89.56molH2 min−1 molCo −1) and good cyclic stability. Experimental results and DFT calculations revealed that the high activity of CoP–CoO/NCDs was due to its novel interface-induced synergistic catalysis mechanism, which simultaneously achieved the decomposition of water and AB molecules. The co-doping of N and P introduced numerous defects that further regulated the reactivity of the carbon layers. This work successfully created a special two-phase interfacial structure of CoO and CoP with great advantages, and provides a new strategy using interface engineering for the design of efficient and inexpensive non-noble-metal catalysts for use in fields related to energy catalysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21905253, 51973200, 51433003 and 21774041), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M640681, 2019T120632), JLU Science and Technology Innovative Research Team 2017TD-06, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Optical Information Materials and Technology (No. 2017B030301007) and the Center of Advanced Analysis & Gene Sequencing, Zhengzhou University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jechem.2020.08.051.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
